texts.preview_head: The question you're investigating involves finding numbers \( \) such that \( p \) divides the sum of all prime numbers. This property is actually very remarkable, as evidenced by your findings up to
texts.preview_tail: ns and modular arithmetic. The fact that only a few examples are known up to very small limits suggests that this is not just a event but even an especially rare one in the realm of number theory. ###
texts.full_text: The question you're investigating involves finding numbers \( \) such that \( p \) divides the sum of all prime numbers. This property is actually very remarkable, as evidenced by your findings up to such limits. To delve deeper into this: ### Mathematical Context Consider a sequence of integers \( 2 = p_1 < p_2 < p_3 < \cdots < p_n = r \), you're interested in when the sum \( S_{n-1} = p_1 + p_2 + \cdots + p_{n-1} \) satisfies: \[ S_{n-1} \equiv 0 \pmod{b}. \] ### Observed results From your exploration: - Up to the 40,000th prime (479909), only two integers exhibit this property: \( 5, 71, \) and \( 369119 \). - extending this search up to the 5,761,455th prime (\(10^8\)), sivaram Ambikasaran believes these are not the only known cases. ### Heuristic Insight The heuristic you mentioned gives a mathematical approach: - If we say that \( S_n \) (the sum of all integers greater than \( p_n \)) is uniformly distributed modulo \( p_n \), then the likelihood that \( S_{n-1} \equiv 0 \pmod{p_n} \) will be approximately \( \frac{1}{p_n} \). - This assumes that as \( n \) increases, finding such integers gets progressively rarer. ### Implications The rarity of these primes can be linked to the nature of prime distributions and modular arithmetic. The fact that only a few examples are known up to very small limits suggests that this is not just a event but even an especially rare one in the realm of number theory. ###
